title-group.article-title: Epidemiological characteristics of rotavirus gastroenteritis in China: A systematic review of studies published from 2014 to 2023

contrib-group.aff: Institute of Medical Biology, Chinese Academy of Medical Science & Peking Union Medical College, Yunnan Key Laboratory of Vaccine Research and Development on Severe Infectious Disease, Kunming, China

author-notes.corresp: Hongjun Li lihj6912@163.com Institute of Medical Biology, Chinese Academy of Medical Science & Peking Union Medical College, Yunnan Key Laboratory of Vaccine Research and Development on Severe Infectious Disease, 935 Jiao Ling Road, Kunming, Yunnan Province 650118, China.

fn.p: These authors contributed equally to this work.

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published with license by Taylor & Francis Group, LLC.

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Rotavirus gastroenteritis (RVGE) is a major public health concern, particularly amongst children below five-years-old. In China, the disease burden remains high owing to the absence of a comprehensive detection system. This study analyses RVGE incidence, population distribution, and pathogenic genotypes reported in studies published between 2014 and 2023, providing evidence to support vaccine and drug development. A systematic review of studies on RVGE published between 2014 and 2023 was conducted. Articles were screened using a standardized algorithm. A random-effects model was employed to analyze rotavirus positive rates and genotype variation trends. Among 320 studies, the highest rotavirus positive rates in children (≤5 y) were in Jilin (48.59%), Shanxi (40.89%), and Xinjiang (37.65%), while the highest number of infections were in Yunnan (89,128), Jiangsu (82,869), and Guangxi (36,524). Among adults (≥15 y), Shaanxi (27.73%), Inner Mongolia (22.95%), and Heilongjiang (17.82%) had the highest rotavirus positive rates, whereas Shanghai (3,170), Beijing (2,019), and Fujian (1,044) recorded the most infections. The rotavirus positive rates were 26.46% and 32.85% for children visiting the outpatient/emergency department and the inpatient department, respectively. Before 2018, the dominant G genotypes were G9 (children: 42.89%; adults: 56.13%) and G3 (children: 20.17%; adults: 14.71%), whereas the dominant P genotype was P[8] (children: 76.30%; adults: 80.78%). After 2018, G9 (children: 66.73%; adults: 67.43%) and P[8] (children: 88.62%; adults: 88.57%) became predominant in both groups, with an increase in G8 (before: 0.89%; after: 5.08%) among children. RVGE remains a significant burden in China, with evolving distributions. Continuous surveillance is essential to guide vaccine strain selection and development.

institution-wrap.institution: the Major Science and Technology Special Project of Yunnan Province (Biomedicine)
Science and Technology Project of Yunnan Province
the Yunnan Talent Support Program for Industrial Technology Leading Talents
the Yunnan Talent Support Program for Top Young Talents

funding-group.funding-statement: This study was supported by funding from the Major Science and Technology Special Project of Yunnan Province (Biomedicine) [202402AA310020, 202202AA100006], Science and Technology Project of Yunnan Province [General Program 202201AT070236], the Yunnan Talent Support Program for Industrial Technology Leading Talents [YNWR-CYJS-2019-017], and the Yunnan Talent Support Program for Top Young Talents [YNWR-QNBJ-2018-390].

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Rotavirus (RV) is a highly contagious virus that can cause severe diarrhea, vomiting, fever, abdominal pain, and even death.1 It belongs to the genus Rotavirus within the Reoviridae family and is a non-enveloped virus with a capsid composed of three concentric protein layers. The outer capsid comprises two proteins, viral protein 7 (VP7) and VP4, while the middle layer consists of VP6, and the inner layer is enclosed by VP2, along with VP1 (an RNA-dependent RNA polymerase) and VP3 (viral capping enzyme). VP4 and VP7 are encoded by different genomic segments, and their neutralizing antibodies are associated with protective immunity.2,3 The P serotype is determined by virus-encoded VP4, a protease (trypsin) cleavage protein that renders RV particles infectious upon cleavage and activation. G serotype specificity refers to the outer capsid glycoprotein VP7.4 Epidemiological studies indicate significant temporal and geographic variations in the prevalence of RV strains. Gl/G2/G3/G4 and P[4]/P[6]/P[8] are the most common dominant strains worldwide. Until the mid-1990s, the four most common combinations were G1P[8], G2P[4], G3P[8], and G4P[6].5

Group A human RVs (RVA), the leading cause of acute gastroenteritis (AGE) in infants worldwide,6 is transmitted via direct contact with an infected person or exposure to fecal matter. Infants and young children are particularly susceptible, often experiencing severe dehydration and even death.7 In low-income countries, RVA remains a major cause of mortality, whereas in developed countries, it significantly contributes morbidity.8,9 Although the administration of RV vaccines has reduced hospitalization rates and mortality, RVA still caused over 128,500 deaths and 258,000,000 diarrheal episodes among children aged below five years globally.10 According to the Chinese Centre for Disease Control and Prevention in 2019, RVA was responsible for approximately 215,000 child deaths worldwide. Therefore, continued epidemiological analysis is essential for developing effective RV vaccines and reasonable treatment methods.

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: Two electronic bibliographic databases were searched: PubMed for English-language literature and CNKI for Chinese-language sources. The PubMed search used the query (((rotavirus) OR (rotaviru*)) AND (China)) AND (“January 1, 2014” [Date – Publication]: “December 31, 2023” [Date – Publication]), restricted to human studies without publication-type filters to capture original epidemiological reports. Correspondingly, CNKI was queried using the controlled subject heading “rotavirus,” with results limited to the period from January 1, 2014 to December 31, 2023 and to Chinese-language publications. Both searches encompassed observational, laboratory-based, and surveillance studies that described rotavirus epidemiology in mainland China. A systematic review approach was adopted to reduce selection and citation biases, extend geographical and temporal coverage, and quantitatively combine heterogeneous evidence, thereby providing an unbiased and current national profile that individual studies or narrative reviews could not offer.

sec.title: Review strategy
sec.p: If the same data appeared in both Chinese and English publications, the English version was prioritized. Two team members independently screened and assessed each study’s quality based on the study design, sample source, scope, case definition, and diagnostic methods using unified standards. Disagreements were resolved through a joint consultation with the corresponding author.

sec.title: Study inclusion and exclusion criteria
sec.p: Studies were included if they met the following criteria: (1) published in either Chinese or English within the specified timeframe, (2) conducted in China, (3) investigated RV as the infectious agent, and (4) provided clear case data on infectious cases. Studies with vague data or those limited to describing case characteristics, treatment methods, pathophysiology, or disease-related mechanisms were excluded from the analysis.

sec.title: Analytical strategy and statistical analysis
sec.p: The study analyzed the spatiotemporal distribution of RV genotypes and their changing prevalence in China. The Qinling Mountains – Huaihe River line, often regarded as the geographical boundary between northern and southern China, was used to distinguish between the two regions. As the included studies were published between 2014 and 2023, covering data from 2006–2022, the majority of detected cases were concentrated between 2011 and 2022. China’s National Viral Diarrhea Surveillance Protocol defines two sentinel hospital populations: children ≤5 y and individuals ≥14 y – so samples from the 5–15-y band are scarce; moreover, published surveillance reports consistently adopt ≥15 y as the adult stratum. RVGE is a major public health concern, particularly amongst children below five-years-old. To enhance epidemiological insights into RVGE, this study categorized participants into two age groups: ≤5 y (children) and ≥15 y (adults). So we could see how the virus behaves in adults and have a reference for comparison. To address potential spectrum bias and improve the interpretability of rotavirus positive rates, we stratified the study population by healthcare setting: outpatient or emergency department visits and inpatient hospitalizations. This stratification was performed separately for children aged ≤5 y and adults aged ≥15 y. Since most rotavirus surveillance in China relies on sentinel hospitals, this approach enables a more nuanced assessment of disease burden across different levels of clinical severity and healthcare utilization. Distinguishing between these settings helps reduce bias from combining populations with differing testing probabilities and baseline infection risks, thus yielding a more accurate representation of rotavirus epidemiology. In 2018, an oral pentavalent reassortant RV-attenuated live vaccine (Vero cells) was approved for marketing in China. To better illustrate recent trends in morbidity and changes in viral genotypes, 2018 was selected as a suitable cutoff for our research analysis.

Owing to the limited research on RV incidence across mainland China, this study retrieved case data based on a retrospective cross-sectional analysis to estimate the prevalence of various RV genotypes. Before integrating the raw data, the Q statistic (p < .10) was used to assess homogeneity among the studies. If substantial heterogeneity was detected, a random-effects model was used; otherwise, a fixed-effects model was used to summarize the combined percentages and 95% confidence intervals (CIs). When fewer than four studies were included in the meta-analysis, the random-effects model provided a point estimate, and the original values were presented as CIs.
Given the heterogeneity in data quality among the retrieved studies, this study prioritized prevalence and rotavirus positive rate from medium/high-quality studies (mean score ≥3).
All statistical analyses were conducted using R language for raw data integration and homogeneity testing. All hypothesis tests were two-sided, with statistical significance set at p < .05.

sec.title: Overview of studies
sec.p:
An initial search identified 3255 and 737 citations related to RV in the CNKI and PubMed databases, respectively. After reviewing the titles and abstracts, duplicate records and research reports unrelated to RVGE were excluded. After reading the full texts, studies with incomplete or unclear data were excluded. Finally, 320 studies met the inclusion criteria (Figure 1). Most studies (81.56%) were published in Chinese. Of these, 208 studies (65.00%) were classified as high quality, each receiving an average score of ≥4.0. Figure 1.
PRISMA flow diagram for study selection.

sec.title: Epidemiological characteristics of RV infection
sec.p:
Based on the final analysis of the 320 articles, this study summarizes RV prevalence trends in mainland China over the past 10–15 y. The data presented in these articles originated from sentinel monitoring hospitals, general hospitals, regional disease prevention and control centers, universities, and other relevant published research. Using epidemiological investigation methods, we found that most studies focused on infants and young children. However, a significant gap remains in the analysis of RV infections in adults. Therefore, this study examined the infection status and nationwide distribution of RVGE in adults. A national distribution map of RVs indicated that infections in children aged ≤5 y predominantly occurred in southern China, particularly in Yunnan, Jiangsu, Guangxi, and Zhejiang, with reported infection cases exceeding 20,000 (Figure 2). Conversely, infections among adults aged ≥15 y were primarily concentrated in Shanghai, Beijing, Fujian, and Guangdong, which are densely populated and economically developed areas (Figure 3). Figure 2.
National map of rotavirus infection in children ≤5-y-old. Figure 3.
National map of rotavirus infection in adult ≥15-y-old.

Statistical data showed that the highest incidence of infection among children were reported in Yunnan (89,128), Jiangsu (82,869), Guangxi (36,524), Zhejiang (34,244), and Guangdong (15,413). The number of infection cases in the children group in other provinces remained below 15,000 (Figure 4). Additionally, we analyzed the rotavirus positive rate across various provinces, identifying Jilin (48.59%), Shanxi (40.89%), Xinjiang (37.65%), Guizhou (35.85%), Henan (35.71%), and Liaoning (35.49%) as having the highest rates, while all other provinces recorded rotavirus positive rate below 35.00% (Figure 4). For the adult group, the highest number of RV infection cases were reported in Shanghai (3,170), Beijing (2,019), Fujian (1,044), Guangdong (866), and Inner Mongolia (498), with fewer than 400 cases recorded in other provinces. The highest rotavirus positive rate among adults were in Shaanxi (27.73%), Inner Mongolia (22.95%), and Heilongjiang (17.82%), whereas all other provinces recorded rates below 15.00% (Figure 5). Furthermore, this study summarized the total number of infection cases across different administrative regions. Among children aged ≤5 y, the highest infection proportions were reported in East China (137,795; 38.80%), Southwest China (97,406; 27.50%), and Central South China (69,900; 19.70%) (Figure 6A), highlighting a higher prevalence in the south and fewer cases in the north in the pediatric group. For adults aged ≥15 y, the highest number of infections were observed in East China (4,802; 53.06%), North China (2,517; 27.81%), and Central South China (1,238; 13.68%) (Figure 6B) and were primarily distributed in the south. Notably, while the highest number of infections among the children group were located in the south, the highest rotavirus positive rate were mostly in northern China. A similar pattern was observed in adults, where infection cases were concentrated in mainly developed and densely populated areas in China. Moreover, as in the pediatric group, the provinces with the highest rotavirus positive rate were also located in northern China. Figure 4.
Rotavirus positive cases in children ≤5 y of age. Figure 5.
Rotavirus positive cases in adult ≥15 y of age. Figure 6.
A in China, based on administrative divisions, the proportion of rotavirus-positive cases among children in each region; B in China, based on administrative divisions, the proportion of rotavirus-positive cases among adult in each region; C annual trends in the rotavirus positive rate of patients with RVGE from 2011–2022. (estimated values and 95% CI were calculated by integrating the rotavirus positive rate reported in the literature for that year).

To further analyze the trend of RV rotavirus positive rate in China over the past decade, we examined 177 of 320 articles that reported the annual case distribution of patients with RVGE. Overall, the rotavirus positive rate was 23.17% (95% CI: 17.86–29.49%) in 2011, remaining above 20% until 2016, when it declined to 20.49% (95% CI: 16.69–24.89%). However, a downward trend was observed in 2017 and 2018, with a gradual decrease each year until it reached 7.65% (95% CI: 4.49–10.81%) in 2022 (Figure 6C). Given the significant influence of healthcare settings on rotavirus positivity rates, this study stratified the population into two major scenarios – outpatient/emergency and inpatient – for hierarchical analysis. Among adults, 33 studies that clearly defined the healthcare setting were included, all of which involved outpatient or emergency patients. The rotavirus positivity rate in adult outpatient/emergency cases was 7.01% (95% CI: 6.83–7.18) (Table 1). In the children group, 67 studies focused on the outpatient/emergency population and 52 involved inpatients. The positivity rate among pediatric outpatient/emergency cases was 26.46% (95% CI: 26.33–26.59), whereas the rate rose to 32.85% (95% CI: 32.60–33.11) among hospitalized children, indicating a notable difference between these groups (Table 1). These findings highlight the healthcare setting as an important variable influencing rotavirus detection in children, while the absence of adult inpatient data underscores the need for related future studies.Table 1.
RV positivity in Chinese people in different population.
CharacteristicsNo. of studiesPooled percentageRV positive cases
%95% CI
Adult Outpatient Department/Emergency Department337.016.83 ~ 7.185,747
Children Outpatient Department/Emergency Department6726.4626.33 ~ 26.59115,622
Children Inpatient department5232.8532.60 ~ 33.1147,789
Total152 169,158

sec.title: Distribution of genotypes
sec.p:
Among the 320 studies analyzed, 76 detailed the genotype distribution of rotavirus, systematically outlining the epidemiological profiles of VP7 (G-type) and VP4 (P-type) across mainland Chinese provinces. To clarify epidemiological differences in RV genotypes between populations, the studies were stratified into two main age groups – children aged ≤5 y and adults aged ≥15 y – and further classified by healthcare setting as “outpatient/emergency” or “inpatient” for subgroup analysis. Only 3 studies in the adult group clearly defined their study populations, all of which focused on outpatient or emergency cases; the children’s group included 15 outpatient/emergency studies and 23 inpatient studies. Analysis of pooled adult outpatient/emergency data showed that the predominant G-types were G9, G1, and G3, while P[8] and P[4] were the dominant P-types (Figure 7A and Table 2). Among children, both in outpatient/emergency and inpatient settings, the top three dominant G-types were consistently G9, G3, and G1, with no significant differences in their proportions. However, the proportion of G8 increased substantially from 0.70% in the outpatient/emergency group to 9.59% in the inpatient group, suggesting a possible link between clinical phenotype and genotype distribution (Figure 7B,C and Tables 3,4). Subsequently, studies were further divided into two time periods – before and after 2018—to evaluate temporal shifts in genotype succession. Specifically, 68 and 11 studies were conducted before and after 2018, respectively. The total number of studies exceeded 76 due to overlapping research in both categories. Additionally, we subdivided the studies within each timeframe based on age, creating two groups: children – aged ≤5 y – and adult – aged ≥15 y – groups. Altogether, 64 and five studies on the children and adult groups, respectively, were performed before 2018, whereas nine and two studies focused on these respective demographics were performed after 2018. Figure 7.
The distribution of rotavirus strains among different populations in China.Table 2.
Distribution of RV genotypes in Chinese adult. (Outpatient department/emergency department).

Since each number is rounded to two decimal places, the sum is approximately 100%.

sec.title: Epidemiology of VP7 subtypes in China
sec.p:
In the adult group, the strains with the highest number of infection cases were G9 (778), G2 (163), and G3 (138) before 2018. Additionally, the strains with dominant proportions were G9 (56.13%; 95% CI: 41.56–70.70%), G3 (14.71%; 95% CI: 4.01–25.40%), and G1 (11.31%; 95% CI: 2.31–20.32%) (Figure 8B and Table 7). After 2018, the strains with the highest number of infections were G9 (118 cases), G1 (19 cases), and G2 (11 cases), with G9 (67.43%; 95% CI: 65.87%–100.00%), G1 (11.38%), and G2 (6.59%) being the dominant strains (Figure 8B and Table 8). Table 7.
Distribution of RV genotypes in Chinese adults before 2018.

sec.title: Epidemiology of VP4 subtypes in China
sec.p:
Before 2018, the total number of RV infections with P genotypes among the children group was 19,349, with P[8] (n = 13,880), P[4] (n = 2,063), and P[11] (n = 299) being the most infectious. The dominant strains based on the proportion of P genotypes were P[8] (76.3%; 95% CI: 71.15–80.78%), P[4] (10.22%; 95% CI: 8.46–12.29%), and P[11] (6.05%; 95% CI: 3.08–9.02%). However, the mixed genotypes were predominantly P[4]P[6] (Figure 9A and Table 4). After 2018, the three most infected groups were P[8] (1,640 cases), P[4] (199 cases), and P[6] (49 cases). Based on the proportion of P genotypes, the dominant strains were P[8] (88.62%; 95% CI: 80.15–93.76%), P[4] (6.7%; 95% CI: 3.77–11.63%), and P[6] (3.98%; 95% CI: 0.66–9.40) (Figure 9A and Table 5). The dominant P genotype remained P[8], and the mixed genotype was predominantly P[4]P[8].Figure 9.
Distribution of the VP4 genotype of rotavirus in children ≤5-y-old; B distribution of the VP4 genotype of rotavirus in adults ≥15-y-old. (b. P[6](1.67%); P[1,8](1.47%); P[6,8](1.19%); P[9](0.89%); P[4,8](0.84%); P[10](0.58%); P[12](0.24%)).

In the adult group, the predominant P genotypes causing infections were P[8] and P[4] in both periods. Compared with the period before 2018, the proportion of P[8] increased to 88.57% (95% CI: 88.02–100.00) after 2018 (Figure 9B and Tables 7,8).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: RV, norovirus, astrovirus, and enteric adenovirus are the primary pathogens responsible for viral gastroenteritis, accounting for over 90% of all cases. Among these, RV is the most prevalent. This study examined the prevalence of RV in China, based on both domestic and international publications spanning from 2014–2023. This extensive timeline allowed for a comprehensive analysis of the epidemiological characteristics of RVs. Among children aged ≤5, Jilin (48.59%), Shanxi (40.89%), and Xinjiang (37.65%) had the highest rotavirus positive rate. However, these provinces are all located in northern China, which contrasts with the top three provinces with the highest number of infections: Yunnan (89128), Jiangsu (82869), and Guangxi (36524), all of which are located in southern China. Studies suggest that a higher humidity and ambient temperature are associated with lower RV rotavirus positive rates. Local climatic factors may be related to the seasonality of RV disease, with the incidence increasing during cool and dry seasons, especially in regions where the annual RV epidemic occurs simultaneously with winter.11,12 Most northern regions of China, delineated by the Qinling – Huaihe Line, fall within the temperate zone. These areas experience cool, dry winters, correlating with higher rotavirus positive rate than those observed in the south. RVGE occurs throughout the year among children ≤5 y in China, with a seasonal peak typically observed from November – February, often referred to as “autumn diarrhoea.” The epidemic peak in the northern provinces occurs slightly earlier than in the southern provinces, with a slightly higher and shorter peak duration.13,14 This may explain the higher total number of infections in southern China than in northern China. Although minor variations in local climate may influence the seasonal cycles of RV disease in certain situations, the overall differences in global seasonal RV disease prevalence cannot be attributed solely to climate.15 Notably, the highest number of RV infections among the adult group were observed in Shanghai (3,170), Beijing (2,019), Fujian (1,044), and Guangdong (866), all of which are densely populated and economically developed regions. Furthermore, regions with higher detection amongst adults included Shaanxi (27.73%), Inner Mongolia (22.95%), and Heilongjiang (17.82%), which, similar to the children group, are located in northern China. Relevant studies have indicated that the mortality rate of RV infection is low in developed regions. However, sporadic outbreaks still occur in high-density settings, including military camps, cruise ships, schools, hospitals, and nursing homes.16,17 Incomplete reporting of RV infections in some regions may contribute to data discrepancies.

A systematic review of 69 national studies conducted between 2011 and 2018, which included both outpatient and inpatient data, found that rotavirus was detected in 34.0% (95% CI: 31.3–36.8) of diarrhea cases among children under five years of age. The review reported a rotavirus positivity rate of 39.7% in hospitalized patients, compared to 23.9% in outpatients, indicating that rotavirus is a leading cause of pediatric hospitalization.18 These findings align with the present study. According to previous research data (2003–2013), the provinces with the highest rotavirus positive rate were Henan (75.20%), Jilin (64.94%), and the Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region (60.16%). Additionally, the highest number of infections were recorded in Guangdong (186,587), Zhejiang (133,390), and Hubei (11,104).19 Studies published in the past decade report significantly lower RV rotavirus positive rate than those from 2003–2013. Similarly, the number of infections in provinces with the highest rotavirus positive rates also declined compared with previous studies. For instance, Zhejiang, which has consistently reported a high number of infection cases, experienced a reduction of nearly 90,000 cases compared to prior studies. In Shanghai, a highly developed area, the timely vaccination rate for the RV vaccine increased from 0.00% in 2011 to 33.96% in 2019.20 Our review identified 1689 cases of RV infection among children aged ≤5 y in Shanghai between 2010 and 2022, marking a decline of nearly 3000 cases compared with the 4730 cases reported in previous studies between 1999 and 2011. Furthermore, RV rotavirus positive rate decreased annually from 2011–2022, particularly after 2017. These trends suggest that expanding the coverage of RV vaccines may play a key role in reducing RV prevalence. Given that the RV vaccine is not included in China’s national immunization program, its coverage is closely related to factors such as regional economy, education level, and disease awareness.21 Future studies should focus on strengthening comprehensive health education interventions targeting knowledge, attitudes, and behaviors to improve timely vaccination rates.

This review study also examined the distribution of G and P genotypes among children aged ≤5 y and adults aged ≥15 y, comparing trends before and after 2018. It highlighted changes over time and included the proportions of the mixed genotypes. A study reported the genotyping of RV diarrhea among children under five years of age in China from 2019–2023. In this age group, the dominant RV genotype was G9, accounting for 85.48% of all cases. Other rare genotypes included G2 (7.70%), G8 (5.74%), G1 (4.86%), and G3 (3.21%). Regarding P types, P[8] was the dominant strain (64.02%), followed by P[4] (10.07%) and P[6] (1.85%),22 which is consistent with our findings. However, from 2003–2007, the number of strains with the G3 serotype decreased, whereas those with the G1 serotype increased. After 2011, the most common strains in China shifted from G3 to G9.23 However, from 2003–2007, the number of strains with the G3 serotype decreased, whereas those with the G1 serotype increased. After 2011, the most common strains in China shifted from G3 to G9.24 Previous studies on articles published between 2003 and 2013 revealed that the top three G genotypes were G3 (8,509 cases), G1 (6,490 cases), and G2 (1,601 cases), whereas the G8 genotype (23 cases, 0.11%) accounted for a much smaller proportion.19 However, in our study, G9 was the dominant genotype affecting children aged ≤5 y. A comparison between the pre- and post-2018 data showed a significant increase in the proportions of G9 (from 42.89% to 66.73%) and G8 (from 0.89% to 5.08%). Conversely, the proportions of the G1 (from 10.67% to 5.84%) and G3 (from 20.17% to 6.28%) genotypes decreased significantly after 2018. Among the P genotypes, P[8] remained the dominant strain; however, its proportion showed a noticeable increase after 2018 (from an unspecified percentage to 82.82%) (Tables 1–2). Rotavirus vaccines Rotarix (G1P[8] monovalent) and RotaTeq (pentavalent: G1/G2/G3/G4/P[8]) are widely used worldwide. Clinical data show protection rates of at least 85% against severe diarrhea, but cross-protection against non-vaccine strains such as G2P[4] and G12P[8] remains limited. Because these vaccines mainly target G1/G2/G3/G4/P[8], their effectiveness may be challenged by the increasing prevalence of emerging strains like G3P[8] and G12P[8], which drives the development of multivalent or updated formulations.25 G9P[8] has evolved from a rare serotype into the fifth most prevalent strain worldwide. It is now commonly identified across Asia, including China, Thailand, India, and Iran, as well as in Africa, Latin America, and Oceania. The latest multivalent and full-genome recombinant vaccines, such as 7/8-valent formulations, incorporate the G9 strain to broaden protection against G9P[8], G9P[4], and G9P[6] genotypes.26 In developing countries, the G8 genotype has been found to circulate and even dominate in several nations, including Malawi and Kenya, typically in combination with P[4], P[6], or P[8].27 Surveillance data from Malawi, Kenya, and South Africa report G8 detection rates of 10–30%, often involving reassortment with gene segments of animal origin, such as from bovine or caprine strains. More recently, G8P[8] or G8P[4] strains have been identified in Thailand, India, Iran, and several Chinese cities including Shanghai and Wuhan. Shanghai’s 2022 surveillance revealed that G8P[8] constituted 37.4% of all positive strains, ranking as the second most prevalent. During the 2021–2022 epidemic season in Wuhan, G8P[8] became the dominant strain.28–31 In earlier years, from 2000 to 2010, G8 was detected only sporadically. While current surveillance data are still limited, reports confirm the emergence of G8P[8] in some regions, highlighting the necessity for enhanced nationwide genotyping surveillance. Strengthening the national genotyping surveillance network – including monitoring of animal-derived strains – and conducting cross-protection assessments after vaccine introduction would facilitate timely adjustments to vaccine formulations or booster strategies.

Our study also had some limitations. First, the data were sourced from sentinel hospitals, provincial and municipal hospitals, disease prevention and control centers, and universities. These institutions are generally located in relatively dense populations with advanced medical education and infrastructure. However, data from remote areas with limited educational and medical developments are scarce, introducing potential bias in characterizing RV infections across different regions, such as northern and southern China. Second, China has a large and highly mobile population; however, relevant data on population mobility and regional flow analyses are lacking. Additionally, owing to provincial research reports, there may be duplicate data in the statistics of positive cases, as the same infection cases might appear in different research reports. When comparing the number of infection cases before and after 2018, biases in the data after 2018 may exist owing to the relatively few published studies on RV infections in recent years. Similarly, biases may exist in adult research data owing to the limited number of related studies. Furthermore, the estimates in this study are based on rotavirus positive rate, which are influenced by detection sensitivity and specificity and may differ from the true incidence/infection rates.

Between January 1, 2008 and December 31, 2016, children ≤5 y admitted to the hospital for acute gastroenteritis were enrolled in the Global RV Detection Network (GRSN). Following the introduction of the RV vaccine, a continuous decline was observed in the proportion of RV-induced acute gastroenteritis among children ≤5 y at the GRSN-affiliated institutions.32 These findings highlight the need for introducing an RV vaccine into national immunization programs and emphasize the importance of high-quality monitoring. Overall, this retrospective study systematically evaluated RV infection trends and rotavirus positive rate in China, emphasizing the need for continuous national epidemiological surveillance. Considering the significant disease burden caused by RV infections, particularly among infants and young children, the use of a vaccine targeting RVs is advisable for controlling viral enteritis in China. Continuous monitoring is crucial for designing novel RV vaccines and improving their effectiveness.

ack.title: Acknowledgment
ack.p: Hongjun Li, Yan Zhou, and Yanwei Wu conceived and designed the study. Yanwei Wu, Xinling Liu, Qingmei Leng, Xiaopeng Song, Jinyuan Wu, and Maosheng Sun participated in screening and scoring. Yanwei Wu and Xinling Liu analyzed the data. Yanwei Wu wrote the manuscript.

bio.p: Yan Zhou, Ph.D., is a Researcher and Master’s Supervisor in Pathogen Biology at Peking Union Medical College (Tsinghua University School of Medicine), specializing in rotavirus with a focus on both fundamental studies and vaccine development. Having led multiple significant research projects, including grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China, the Major New Drug Innovation Project of Yunnan Province, and the Yunnan Provincial Basic Research General Program. She has also contributed extensively to several other national and provincial-level major initiatives. Research accomplishments include multiple SCI publications as first or corresponding author, alongside 7 authorized national invention patents (one international). Recognized for this work, she has received the Yunnan Provincial Science and Technology Progress Award (Third Prize) and multiple provincial and municipal-level talent titles.

Hongjun Li is the head of the Molecular Biology Laboratory at the Institute of Medical Biology, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences, and a doctoral supervisor at the Peking Union Medical College. He is recognized as a leading young academic and technical talent in Yunnan Province, as well as a “Leading Talent in Industrial Technology” under the Yunnan Province Ten Thousand Talent Plan. Dr. Li focuses on viral vaccine research and development, overseeing an inactivated rotavirus vaccine currently in Phase II clinical trials. He has published more than 70 papers, including more than 30 SCI articles, in prominent domestic and international journals. Additionally, he holds seven invention patents and has received the Yunnan Province Science and Technology Progress Award four times.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: The materials and datasets analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

sec.title: Declaration of generative AI in scientific writing
sec.p: The authors declare that no generative artificial intelligence (AI) or AI-assisted technologies were used in this manuscript.

sec.title: Ethical statement
sec.p: This study does not involve human participants, and therefore, ethical approval was not required.
